[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/NFL_Rams_vs_Panthers_2010.jpg/250px-NFL_Rams_vs_Panthers_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:NFL_Rams_vs_Panthers_2010.jpg)

[St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") at home to [Carolina](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") in week 8 of the season, on October 31, 2010

The [2010 season](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season") was the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams")' 73rd in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), and their 16th in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"). The team greatly improved on their near winless record of 1–15 from the [2009 season](/wiki/2009_St._Louis_Rams_season "2009 St. Louis Rams season") by achieving more total victories than in their previous three seasons combined for a record of 7–9, which almost won a weak [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") ultimately decided by a loss to the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2010_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2010 Seattle Seahawks season") in the final week. Since the start of the [2007 NFL season](/wiki/2007_NFL_season "2007 NFL season"), the Rams had won just 6 combined games and had not qualified for the playoffs since [2004](/wiki/2004_St._Louis_Rams_season "2004 St. Louis Rams season"). In the [2010 NFL draft](/wiki/2010_NFL_draft "2010 NFL draft"), the Rams had the number 1 overall pick, which they picked [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") from Oklahoma. This year marked their second season under head coach [Steve Spagnuolo](/wiki/Steve_Spagnuolo "Steve Spagnuolo"). The Rams played all of their home games at the [Edward Jones Dome](/wiki/Edward_Jones_Dome "Edward Jones Dome"), in St. Louis, Missouri.

The Rams fired two trainers (Jim Anderson and Dake Walden), and assistant line coach [Art Valero](/wiki/Art_Valero "Art Valero") left for the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[Stan Kroenke](/wiki/Stan_Kroenke "Stan Kroenke") was approved by the NFL on August 25, 2010, to purchase 100% of the Rams from the current owners [Chip Rosenbloom](/wiki/Chip_Rosenbloom "Chip Rosenbloom") and Lucia Rodriguez. Kroenke took control of the team before the start of the 2010 season. He also owns the [Denver Nuggets](/wiki/Denver_Nuggets "Denver Nuggets") of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"), as well as the [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche") of the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Offseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Offseason")]

### Key departures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Key departures")]

Quarterback [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger") was released by the Rams on April 5, 2010.

### Key additions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Key additions")]

### 2010 NFL draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2010 NFL draft")]

Main article: [2010 NFL draft](/wiki/2010_NFL_draft "2010 NFL draft")

The Rams picked first in the first round after finishing the 2009 season with the worst overall record at 1–15. In addition, the Rams had a fifth round pick from the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") from a trade that sent LB [Will Witherspoon](/wiki/Will_Witherspoon "Will Witherspoon") to the Eagles, and a seventh round pick from the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") from a trade that sent CB [Tye Hill](/wiki/Tye_Hill "Tye Hill") to the Falcons.

The Rams selected [University of Oklahoma](/wiki/University_of_Oklahoma "University of Oklahoma") [QB](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") with their 2010 number 1 overall pick.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The St. Louis Rams began their season at home for an NFC West match against the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals"). The Rams trailed in the 2nd quarter with Cardinals kicker [Jay Feely](/wiki/Jay_Feely "Jay Feely") nailing a 22-yard field goal, which was replied when Rams kicker [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)") made a 46-yard field goal. The Rams fell behind again with running back [Tim Hightower](/wiki/Tim_Hightower "Tim Hightower") making a 1-yard TD run, but were able to score with rookie quarterback [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") making a 1-yard TD pass to wide receiver [Laurent Robinson](/wiki/Laurent_Robinson "Laurent Robinson"). In the third quarter, the Rams took the lead for the first time when Brown nailed a 25-yard field goal, but it went downhill in the fourth quarter when quarterback [Derek Anderson](/wiki/Derek_Anderson_(American_football) "Derek Anderson (American football)") made a 21-yard TD pass to wide receiver [Larry Fitzgerald](/wiki/Larry_Fitzgerald "Larry Fitzgerald"), giving St. Louis a loss.

With the loss, St. Louis began the season at 0–1.

Hoping to rebound from their divisional loss to the Cardinals the Rams flew to [Oakland–Alameda County Coliseum](/wiki/Oakland%E2%80%93Alameda_County_Coliseum "Oakland–Alameda County Coliseum") for an Interconference duel with the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders"). In the 2nd quarter the Rams would score first with QB [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") making a 7-yard TD pass to WR [Mark Clayton](/wiki/Mark_Clayton_(American_football,_born_1982) "Mark Clayton (American football, born 1982)"). Then the Raiders replied with kicker [Sebastian Janikowski](/wiki/Sebastian_Janikowski "Sebastian Janikowski") nailing a 38-yard field goal; then he booted a 41-yard field goal in the third quarter. Then the Rams fell behind when QB [Bruce Gradkowski](/wiki/Bruce_Gradkowski "Bruce Gradkowski") made a 4-yard TD pass to WR [Louis Murphy](/wiki/Louis_Murphy "Louis Murphy"), followed in the fourth quarter by Janikowski nailing a 22-yard field goal. The Rams cut the lead when Bradford found WR [Mark Clayton](/wiki/Mark_Clayton_(American_football,_born_1982) "Mark Clayton (American football, born 1982)") on a 17-yard TD pass, but couldn't pull out the victory.

With the close loss and 13th straight loss to an AFC opponent, the Rams fell to 0–2.

After two close losses the Rams hoped to bounce back at the [Edward Jones Dome](/wiki/Edward_Jones_Dome "Edward Jones Dome") for their first win of the season. On the first possession the Rams drove down the field and capped off their drive with a 41-yard touchdown run by [Steven Jackson](/wiki/Steven_Jackson "Steven Jackson"). On the ensuing Redskins drive, [Donovan McNabb](/wiki/Donovan_McNabb "Donovan McNabb") drove into Rams territory before [Na'il Diggs](/wiki/Na%27il_Diggs "Na'il Diggs") forced a fumble on [Santana Moss](/wiki/Santana_Moss "Santana Moss"). [James Butler](/wiki/James_Butler_(American_football) "James Butler (American football)"), who was playing for an injured [Craig Dahl](/wiki/Craig_Dahl "Craig Dahl") picked up the fumbled and returned it 49 yards before being forced out of bounds. Bradford threw a three-yard touchdown pass to [Daniel Fells](/wiki/Daniel_Fells "Daniel Fells") to make the score 14–0. The Redskins tried to start their offense again, but after being stopped on third down had to punt. [Graham Gano](/wiki/Graham_Gano "Graham Gano")'s punt was blocked however by Rams rookie wide receiver [Dominique Curry](/wiki/Dominique_Curry "Dominique Curry"), and the Rams took over in striking distance for another touchdown. Sam Bradford threw his first interception of the day, and the Redskins were able to take over again, and were able to score a field goal. On the kickoff after the Redskins field goal, [Mardy Gilyard](/wiki/Mardy_Gilyard "Mardy Gilyard"), the Rams rookie kick returner/wide receiver fumbled the ball, and the Redskins took over on the 21 yard line. Donovan McNabb hit Santana Moss for a touchdown the next play. Steven Jackson was injured on the next drive for the Rams, and the Rams were unable to score. The next drive the Redskins kicked another field goal to make the score 13–14. The Rams marched down the field one last time, but were stalled in the redzone. With 31 seconds left [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)") attempted a field goal, but the Redskins blocked the kick and at the half the Rams led 14–13. At the start of the second half, McNabb hit Moss for a 56-yard pass. The Rams kept the Redskins out of the endzone, and Washington settled for the go ahead field goal to make the score 16–14. The next drive St. Louis marched down the field, and [Kenneth Darby](/wiki/Kenneth_Darby "Kenneth Darby"), playing for an injured Steven Jackson scored his first TD of the year from 12 yards out to make the score 21–16. Redskins were unable to score during the fourth quarter however, and McNabb was picked off by [Bradly Fletcher](/w/index.php?title=Bradly_Fletcher&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bradly Fletcher (page does not exist)") as he attempted a comeback. Josh Brown kicked three field goals in the final quarter to seal the Rams first victory of the year with a score of 30–16, snapping a 13-game home losing skid. This win also marked their highest scoring performance in a regular season game since their 34–14 victory over the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") on October 19, 2008.

With the win, the Rams improved to 1–2.

The Rams' fourth game was played at home in an NFC west rivalry match against the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks"). In the first quarter the Rams took the early lead as QB [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") completed a 15-yard TD pass to WR [Brandon Gibson](/wiki/Brandon_Gibson "Brandon Gibson"). The Seahawks cut the lead with kicker [Olindo Mare](/wiki/Olindo_Mare "Olindo Mare") nailing a 22-yard field goal, but the Rams increased their lead when kicker [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)") made a 30-yard field goal, followed in the 3rd quarter by Bradford completing a 21-yard TD pass to RB [Kenneth Darby](/wiki/Kenneth_Darby "Kenneth Darby"). Then [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)") made a 31-yard field goal.

With the victory, the Rams improved to 2–2, thus successfully improving on their previous season. The win also snapped a 10-game regular season losing streak to the Seahawks.

Hoping to increase their current winning streak the Rams flew to [Ford Field](/wiki/Ford_Field "Ford Field") for an NFC duel with the Lions. The Rams trailed early with kicker [Jason Hanson](/wiki/Jason_Hanson "Jason Hanson") making a 30-yard field goal, but the Rams replied with kicker [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)") making a 28-yard field goal. In the second quarter the Rams struggled as Hanson's kick was returned 105 yards to the endzone by WR [Stefan Logan](/wiki/Stefan_Logan "Stefan Logan"), followed by QB [Shaun Hill](/wiki/Shaun_Hill "Shaun Hill") making a 1-yard TD pass to WR [Calvin Johnson](/wiki/Calvin_Johnson "Calvin Johnson"). The Rams tried to cut the lead with Brown nailing another 28-yard field goal, but they continued to struggle when Hill found TE [Brandon Pettigrew](/wiki/Brandon_Pettigrew "Brandon Pettigrew") on a 3-yard TD pass and then in the 3rd quarter he found WR [Nate Burleson](/wiki/Nate_Burleson "Nate Burleson") on a 26-yard TD pass. In the fourth quarter the Rams had more problems when they gave up a 48 and a 47-yard field goal by Hanson and an interception by DB [Alphonso Smith](/wiki/Alphonso_Smith "Alphonso Smith") in which he returned 42 yards to the endzone for a touchdown.

With the loss, the Rams fell to 2–3.

The decision was made at overtime when Nedney successfully put away a 29-yard field goal to give the Rams a loss, bringing their record down to 4–5.

They trailed for the first time with Smith completing a 60-yard TD pass to WR [Michael Crabtree](/wiki/Michael_Crabtree "Michael Crabtree"). They got the lead back with Brown getting a 30-yard field goal, followed by QB [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford") getting a 3-yard TD pass to WR [Laurent Robinson](/wiki/Laurent_Robinson "Laurent Robinson"). The lead was narrowed with kicker [Jeff Reed](/wiki/Jeff_Reed_(American_football) "Jeff Reed (American football)") nailing a 30-yard field goal, but the Rams pulled away with Brown making a 28-yard field goal.

With the win, the Rams improved to 7–8.

Coming off their win over the 49ers, the Rams flew to [Qwest Field](/wiki/Qwest_Field "Qwest Field") for a Week 17 NFC West rematch with the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks"), with the division title on the line. St. Louis trailed early in the first quarter as Seahawks quarterback [Charlie Whitehurst](/wiki/Charlie_Whitehurst "Charlie Whitehurst") completed a 4-yard touchdown pass to wide receiver [Mike Williams](/wiki/Mike_Williams_(wide_receiver,_born_1984) "Mike Williams (wide receiver, born 1984)"). The Rams answered in the second quarter with a 32-yard field goal from kicker [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)").

St. Louis chopped away as their deficit in the third quarter with a 27-yard field goal from Brown, but Seattle replied with kicker [Olindo Mare](/wiki/Olindo_Mare "Olindo Mare") making a 31-yard field goal. Afterwards, the Seahawks pulled away in the fourth quarter with Mare getting a 38-yard and a 34-yard field goal.

With the loss, the Rams' season came to an end at 7–9.
